o.leid.31

786017

            

Syene

unspecified

152-145

phrourarchos

Herodes, son of Demophon, from Pergamo; one of the diadochoi, hegemon of the men, phrourarchos of Syene, gerrophulax (leader of soldiers carrying wicker-work shields), tetagmenos (appointed over) the upper territories, prophet of Chnoubis, and archistolistes (chief temple attendant) of the shrines in Elephantine, Abaton, and Philai.

OGIS 111 = Bernand 1989, 302

Messene, Akra

phroura (theoretical)

213

unspecified

Philip V wanted to seize the akra and establish a phroura there following a sacrifice, but the elder Aratus dissuaded him. On this occasion Aratus figuratively used phroura to describe the good faith of the allies that Antigonus handed down to him.1 This is an interesting literal and figurative use of the term within the same context.

Plb. 7.12.5-7

phrourion

1st centruy

unspecified

M. Schottky, Media Atropatene und Gross-Armenien in hellenistischer Zeit (Bonn 1989, 1989 ) , 15—27 , - Ouera should, according to Schottky, be the town named Phraata or Phraaspa, the site of which is unknown. Perhaps the likeliest identification is with the ruins near the village of Lailan.; somewhat close to this pid

Budorus / Boudaron

phrourion

428

unspecified

Diodoros mentions the garrison at 12.49.2 (428 BC). This passage summarises Thucydides 2.93 where, however, the Athenian garrison on Salamis on a cape facing Megara remains unnamed. At 3.51.2 Thucydides mentions the same garrison and this time names it. Using Salamis here. Spartans seem to have given up the fort after a day.
